title: Idiopathic Intracranial Hypertension
oneLiner: Elevated CSF pressure without mass/hydrocephalus, predominantly in young obese women; key threat is irreversible vision loss.

sections.Idiopathic Intracranial Hypertension (II: **One-liner:** Elevated CSF pressure without mass/hydrocephalus, predominantly in young obese women; key threat is irreversible vision loss.
sections.1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You:
**Classic vignette:** A 28-year-old woman with BMI 38 presents to ED with a 3-week history of daily, diffuse, pressure-like headache that is worse when lying flat and associated with brief episodes of "graying out" of vision lasting 10–15 seconds, especially when bending over. She also hears a whooshing sound in her left ear that syncs with her pulse. Fundoscopy reveals bilateral optic disc swelling. She is awake, alert, with no focal neurology.

**Dissecting the vignette:**
- **Young obese woman** → strongest epidemiological clue (90% female, BMI >30 in 80%)
- **Headache worse on lying down** → suggests raised ICP; traction on meningeal vessels and cranial nerves (V, IX, X) is position-sensitive
- **Transient visual obscurations** → postural phenomenon due to transient ischemia of swollen optic nerve head; classic "graying out" lasting seconds
- **Pulsatile tinnitus** → high-pressure CSF pulsations transmitted to the venous sinuses; pathognomonic when unilateral and synchronous with pulse
- **Bilateral papilledema** → direct sign of raised ICP transmitted along optic nerve sheath; may be asymmetric
- **No focal neurology** → rules out mass lesion or stroke; supports idiopathic cause

**Atypical presentations:**
- **Male phenotype:** Often leaner, higher risk of secondary causes (e.g., venous sinus thrombosis – always check MRV in men)
- **Severe headache without papilledema** – IIH can present with headache alone (20-30% have no disc swelling initially); requires high index of suspicion and LP
- **Visual loss as first symptom** – rare but devastating; often bilateral and field-based (arcuate defects, enlarged blind spot); may be mistaken for optic neuritis
sections.2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Ma:
**Plain-language definition:** Idiopathic intracranial hypertension (IIH) is a condition of chronically elevated pressure inside the skull that damages the optic nerves, with no identifiable brain tumor, clot, or fluid obstruction. It is essentially "benign" (no direct brain compression) but "malignant" for vision.

**Why it matters:**
- **Frequency:** Incidence ~1-2 per 100,000 in general, but 15-20 per 100,000 in obese women of childbearing age (epidemic rise with obesity). You will see it in ED, neurology, ophthalmology, and even general medicine.
- **Stakes:** Up to 25% of patients suffer permanent vision loss (often asymptomatic until advanced). Headache morbidity is high. Misdiagnosis as migraine or tension headache delays LP and vision-sparing treatment.
- **Common pitfalls:** Not measuring opening pressure during LP (must be done with patient in lateral decubitus, legs extended, relaxed); attributing papilledema to hypertension alone; ignoring pulsatile tinnitus.
sections.3. Pathophysiology — The \"Why\" Behind:
**The story of IIH development:**

The core problem is **impaired CSF absorption** at the arachnoid granulations (or, in some cases, elevated cerebral venous sinus pressure). Normally, CSF is produced at ~0.3-0.5 mL/min and absorbed into the venous system via arachnoid villi. In IIH, for reasons still debated, absorption is functionally reduced. This leads to a state of **CSF overaccumulation** (not overproduction – production is normal).

- **Why obesity?** Adipose tissue secretes multiple cytokines and hormones that alter CSF dynamics. Increased intra-abdominal pressure elevates central venous pressure, which in turn increases cerebral venous sinus pressure, reducing the pressure gradient for CSF absorption. Weight loss >10% can restore normal dynamics.
- **Why women?** Hormonal factors (androgen-to-estrogen ratio, leptin) influence arachnoid granulation function. Some cases are triggered by oral contraceptives, tetracyclines, or vitamin A derivatives – these drugs may alter CSF outflow resistance.

**Mechanism → key clinical sign:**

| Pathophys mechanism | Clinical sign | Why? |
|----------------------|---------------|------|
| Elevated CSF pressure transmitted to optic nerve sheath | Papilledema | Axoplasmic flow stasis at optic nerve head → swelling of nerve fibers → visible blurred disc margins, obliterated cup, hemorrhages |
| Stretching of meninges (tentorium, falx) by high-pressure CSF | Headache worse on lying flat | Meningeal traction increased when recumbent; pain fibers in cranial nerves (V, IX, X) are irritated |
| Transient ischemia of swollen optic nerve head | Transient visual obscurations | Postural drop in perfusion pressure across a swollen disc → temporary "gray-out" |
| High-pressure CSF pulsations transmitted to venous sinuses | Pulsatile tinnitus | CSF pressure wave mechanically compresses the transverse sinus, creating turbulent flow sound in middle ear |
| Chronic high ICP → shear stress on pituitary stalk | Endocrine disturbance (rare) | Can cause mild hyperprolactinemia (menstrual irregularities) or growth hormone suppression |
sections.4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins):
| Category | Cause | Stem clue |
|----------|-------|-----------|
| **Idiopathic (most common)** | No identifiable cause | Young obese female, no meds, no thrombosis |
| **Medication-related** | Tetracyclines (doxycycline, minocycline), vitamin A/retinoids (isotretinoin), lithium, growth hormone, some oral contraceptives | "Started minocycline for acne 2 months ago" |
| **Venous sinus thrombosis** | Thrombus in transverse/sigmoid sinus → impaired CSF absorption | Sudden, severe headache + focal signs? Suspect if male, lean, or hypercoagulable state (OCP, pregnancy, malignancy, Factor V Leiden). MRV essential |
| **Systemic disease** | Obstructive sleep apnea, Addison's disease, hypoparathyroidism, chronic kidney disease (with anemia) | Snoring, daytime sleepiness; hyperpigmentation (Addison's); tetany (hypopara) |
| **Mass lesion mimicker** | Brain tumor, hydrocephalus, chronic meningitis | Focal signs, progressive headache, altered GCS, seizures – these are not IIH! |

**Most common cause:** Idiopathic (hence the name), but always exclude venous sinus thrombosis before labelling as such.
sections.5. Investigations — What, Why, What It M:
**Diagnostic criteria:** Modified Dandy criteria (2013) – must have:
- Symptoms of raised ICP (headache, pulsatile tinnitus, transient visual obscurations)
- Signs (papilledema) – note: can be absent in some cases
- Normal neuroimaging (CT/MRI) with no mass, hydrocephalus, or structural lesion
- Normal CSF composition (no cells, normal protein/glucose)
- Elevated CSF opening pressure: **≥250 mmH₂O** (some sources use >200 in symptomatic, but >250 is diagnostic in children/adults)

**Investigations step-by-step:**

1. **MRI brain with MR venography (MRV)** – First step in ED if papilledema found.
   - **What it shows:** Excludes mass, hydrocephalus, empty sella (common in IIH but not diagnostic), flattened pituitary, distended optic nerve sheath, transverse sinus stenosis (often present, can be primary or secondary). MRV to exclude venous sinus thrombosis.
   - **Why:** Must rule out a cause requiring neurosurgery. MRV is mandatory – missed thrombosis is a medico-legal risk.

2. **Lumbar puncture (LP) with opening pressure** – Must be done immediately if imaging normal.
   - **How:** Lateral decubitus, legs extended, patient relaxed. Measure opening pressure with manometer. Record CSF for cell count, protein, glucose.
   - **Interpretation:** Opening pressure >250 mmH₂O confirms IIH (if CSF normal). If pressure 200-250, consider borderline. Normal CSF composition rules out meningitis or subarachnoid hemorrhage.
   - **Pattern recognition:** The single finding that confirms IIH is **elevated opening pressure with normal imaging and normal CSF**. Normal pressure does NOT rule out – IIH can be intermittent. Repeat if strong suspicion.

3. **Formal visual fields (Humphrey perimetry)** – Baseline and serial monitoring.
   - **What it shows:** Enlarged blind spot (early), arcuate defects, nasal step, concentric constriction. Vision loss is often silent until late.

4. **Lumbar puncture opening pressure revisited:** Many juniors forget to measure opening pressure. **Do not disconnect the manometer too early** – wait 60 seconds for stabilization. Readings in sitting are unreliable (gravity adds ~20 cmH₂O).
sections.6. Management — Approach & Reasoning:
**Immediate (first hour, usually in ED/neurology assessment)**
- **Therapeutic lumbar puncture** – Drain 20-30 mL CSF until closing pressure <200 mmH₂O. This provides immediate headache relief and reduces papilledema. Do not delay if vision threatened.
- **Start acetazolamide** 500 mg PO BD (or 1g IV if severe). Mechanism: carbonic anhydrase inhibitor reduces CSF production by ~50%. Goal: 1-2 g/day, max 4 g/day. Side effects: metabolic acidosis, paresthesias, metallic taste, renal stones.
- **Analgesia** – Paracetamol 1g QDS, avoid opioids (can lower threshold for nausea and mask worsening headache). If severe headache, consider IV diuretics (furosemide 40mg) as adjunct to reduce ICP.

**Ward (24-72 hours)**
- **Monitor visual acuity, colour vision, and formal visual fields** daily. Any deterioration (drop of >2 lines on Snellen, new field defect) → urgent ophthalmology/neurosurgery consult.
- **Optimize acetazolamide dose** – escalate by 500 mg every 2-3 days until response or intolerance. Switch to topiramate 25 mg BD (also carbonic anhydrase inhibitor, plus headache prophylaxis) if acetazolamide side effects intolerable.
- **Weight loss counselling** – Start now. Goal: 5-10% body weight over 6 months. Refer to obesity clinic / dietitian. This is the only disease-modifying treatment.
- **Consider stopping exacerbating drugs** – tetracyclines, retinoids, lithium.

**Long-term (outpatient follow-up)**
- **Medical management continues** – acetazolamide or topiramate for 6-12 months, then taper if vision stable and weight reduced. Repeat LP if symptoms recur.
- **Surgical options (when medical fails):**
  - **Optic nerve sheath fenestration** (faster, for vision loss)
  - **CSF shunting (lumboperitoneal or ventriculoperitoneal)** – for refractory headache and ICP. Risk of shunt infection, obstruction, overdrainage.
  - **Venous sinus stenting** (if transverse sinus stenosis confirmed on venography) – emerging therapy.
- **Pitfalls to avoid:**
  - Do **NOT** use steroids – they can increase ICP and worsen outcomes (paradoxical effect).
  - Do **NOT** delay LP for a minor headache – vision loss is painless and progressive.
  - Do **NOT** discharge without visual field baseline.
  - Do **NOT** co-prescribe acetazolamide with topiramate (additive acidosis risk) – triage to one.
sections.7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison T:
| Feature | IIH | Cerebral Venous Sinus Thrombosis (CVST) | Chronic Migraine (without aura) | Brain Tumor (e.g., meningioma) | Subarachnoid Hemorrhage |
|---------|-----|-----------------------------------------|----------------------------------|---------------------------------|--------------------------|
| **Headache** | Diffuse, worse lying flat, pulsatile tinnitus | Sudden, thunderclap, may be progressive | Unilateral, throbbing, photophonophobia, unilateral | Progressive, morning vomiting, worse with cough | Sudden, worst-ever, "thunderclap" |
| **Papilledema** | Bilateral, often symmetric | Unilateral or bilateral (if later thrombosis) | Absent | May be unilateral if tumor compresses optic nerve | Absent (unless massive bleed) |
| **Imaging (CT/MRI)** | Normal brain, empty sella, left transverse sinus stenosis | Hyperdense sinus, "empty delta sign", edema on CTV | Normal | Mass effect, contrast enhancement | Subarachnoid/ventricular blood |
| **CSF OP** | >250 mmH₂O | Normal or elevated (but often >200) | Normal | Normal (unless hydrocephalus) | Normal or elevated (with blood) |
| **CSF composition** | Normal | May have RBCs, protein elevated | Normal | Normal | Consistent with blood (xanthochromia) |
| **Key differentiating feature** | Normal CSF, female obesity, no history of hypercoagulability | MRV shows thrombus; risk factors (OCP, pregnancy, thrombophilia) | Hx of migrations, no papilledema, normal OP | Focal neurology, mass on imaging | Subarachnoid blood on CT, thunderclap onset |
sections.8. Complications — What If You Miss It?:
**Permanent vision loss** – The most feared complication.
- **How it presents:** Initially asymptomatic visual field defects (arcuate, nasal step). Central vision preserved until late. Then: severe field constriction, acuity drop, eventually optic atrophy (disc becomes pale, swelling resolves).
- **Key signs:** Reduced colour vision, afferent pupillary defect if asymmetric, enlarged blind spot. Once optic atrophy sets in, intervention cannot reverse.
- **What to do:** Urgent LP, high-dose acetazolamide, neurosurgical referral for optic nerve sheath fenestration within hours.

**Headache disability** – Chronic headache affects quality of life and work.
- **Presentation:** Daily tension-like or migraine-like headache despite medical therapy. May persist even after ICP normalized.
- **Key signs:** No papilledema, normal opening pressure during exacerbation. May indicate central sensitization.
- **What to do:** Multidisciplinary approach (pain clinic, amitriptyline, topiramate, avoidance of medication overuse).

**False localizing signs** – Rare.
- **Examples:** Sixth nerve palsy (bilateral or unilateral) due to stretch over petrous ridge. Transient symptoms resolve with ICP lowering.

**Late-presentation pattern (the "miss" stem):**
> A 45-year-old obese woman presents with progressive vision loss in one eye over 6 months. Fundoscopy reveals pale discs with sharp margins. She recalls a headache that resolved spontaneously. Opening pressure is normal. This is **end-stage IIH** with optic atrophy. No treatment can restore vision. The headache resolved because ICP normalized after permanent damage. Tragic but testable.
sections.9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls:
**Mnemonics:**

**"VOICE"** – The four cardinal symptoms of IIH (classic stem set-up):
- **V** – Visual obscurations (transient)
- **O** – Obesity
- **I** – Intracranial headache (worse on lying down)
- **C** – Cranial nerve palsy (VI most common)
- **E** – Ear (pulsatile tinnitus)

**"DON'T BE FLAT"** – What to avoid when suspecting IIH:
- **D** – Delay LP
- **O** – Omit MRVenogram (miss thrombosis)
- **N** – No steroids (they worsen)
- **T** – Treat BP instead of ICP (hypertensive papilledema vs IIH)
- **B** – Bypass formal visual fields
- **E** – Expect normal pressure in all cases (intermittent elevation)
- **FLAT** – Fontanelle flat? Not relevant in adults – but helps in kids.

**Clinical Pearls:**
1. **"If the disc is swollen, always check the opposite eye"** – IIH almost always bilateral; unilateral papilledema suggests tumor or thrombosis.
2. **Pulsatile tinnitus that disappears when you compress the jugular vein** (S.S. sign) suggests high-flow venous sinus stenosis – common in IIH.
3. **Opening pressure ≥250 mmH₂O is diagnostic but treat pressures >200 if symptomatic** – some patients have symptomatic IIH with pressure 200-250, especially if they have empty sella or tortuous optic sheath.
4. **Weight loss >10% cures about 60% of mild-to-moderate IIH** – this is the only disease-modifying intervention. Acetazolamide buys time.
5. **Do not measure opening pressure in sitting** – you'll get falsely high (by ~15-20 cmH₂O). Always decubitus.
6. **IIH can cause low-pressure headache too** – after LP, overdrainage → spinal headache (worse sitting, better lying). Differentiate from recurrence.
7. **Beware of "pseudopapilledema" (optic disc drusen)** – these are buried deposits in optic nerve head that mimic swelling. Use ultrasound or autofluorescence to distinguish. IIH papilledema shows spontaneous venous pulsations in early stages (absent in drusen).

**Common ward mistakes:**
- Applying anti-hypertensives to "treat the headache" – BP is not elevated in IIH unless pain-induced.
- Ordering CT head and stopping (forgetting MRV) – you miss thrombosis.
- Giving steroids "to reduce swelling" – lethal mistake that increases ICP.
- Discharging a patient with new papilledema without an LP – a classic exam failure.
sections.10. Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior (neurology / ophthalmology / neurosurgery) immediately if:**
  - **Sudden vision loss** (acuity drop >2 lines Snellen or new field defect) – needs surgical fenestration within hours.
  - **GCS drop <15** – suggests herniation (rare in IIH but possible if tonometry is extreme >500).
  - **Seizure** – think venous sinus thrombosis extension or cortical vein thrombosis (call stroke team).
  - **Headache unresponsive to LP and acetazolamide** after 24 hours – consider shunt or stenting.
  - **NEWS2 ≥5** (especially if new neurological sign or lactate >2) – rule out sepsis or meningitis (coincident infection can worsen ICP).

- **When to call ICU:**
  - **GCS ≤8** (needs airway protection and ICP monitoring).
  - **Intractable vomiting** with electrolytes disturbance.
  - **Severe metabolic acidosis** from acetazolamide (pH <7.2, HCO₃ <12) – stop drug, give bicarbonate, consider topiramate instead.

- **When to call crash team:**
  - **Cardiorespiratory arrest** (likely unrelated to IIH, but can follow vomiting with aspiration).
  - **Sudden loss of consciousness** (consider acute hydrocephalus from shunt malfunction in known IIH patients with VP shunts).

rawMarkdown:
## Idiopathic Intracranial Hypertension (IIH)

**One-liner:** Elevated CSF pressure without mass/hydrocephalus, predominantly in young obese women; key threat is irreversible vision loss.

### 1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You'll See

**Classic vignette:** A 28-year-old woman with BMI 38 presents to ED with a 3-week history of daily, diffuse, pressure-like headache that is worse when lying flat and associated with brief episodes of "graying out" of vision lasting 10–15 seconds, especially when bending over. She also hears a whooshing sound in her left ear that syncs with her pulse. Fundoscopy reveals bilateral optic disc swelling. She is awake, alert, with no focal neurology.

**Dissecting the vignette:**
- **Young obese woman** → strongest epidemiological clue (90% female, BMI >30 in 80%)
- **Headache worse on lying down** → suggests raised ICP; traction on meningeal vessels and cranial nerves (V, IX, X) is position-sensitive
- **Transient visual obscurations** → postural phenomenon due to transient ischemia of swollen optic nerve head; classic "graying out" lasting seconds
- **Pulsatile tinnitus** → high-pressure CSF pulsations transmitted to the venous sinuses; pathognomonic when unilateral and synchronous with pulse
- **Bilateral papilledema** → direct sign of raised ICP transmitted along optic nerve sheath; may be asymmetric
- **No focal neurology** → rules out mass lesion or stroke; supports idiopathic cause

**Atypical presentations:**
- **Male phenotype:** Often leaner, higher risk of secondary causes (e.g., venous sinus thrombosis – always check MRV in men)
- **Severe headache without papilledema** – IIH can present with headache alone (20-30% have no disc swelling initially); requires high index of suspicion and LP
- **Visual loss as first symptom** – rare but devastating; often bilateral and field-based (arcuate defects, enlarged blind spot); may be mistaken for optic neuritis

### 2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Matters)

**Plain-language definition:** Idiopathic intracranial hypertension (IIH) is a condition of chronically elevated pressure inside the skull that damages the optic nerves, with no identifiable brain tumor, clot, or fluid obstruction. It is essentially "benign" (no direct brain compression) but "malignant" for vision.

**Why it matters:**
- **Frequency:** Incidence ~1-2 per 100,000 in general, but 15-20 per 100,000 in obese women of childbearing age (epidemic rise with obesity). You will see it in ED, neurology, ophthalmology, and even general medicine.
- **Stakes:** Up to 25% of patients suffer permanent vision loss (often asymptomatic until advanced). Headache morbidity is high. Misdiagnosis as migraine or tension headache delays LP and vision-sparing treatment.
- **Common pitfalls:** Not measuring opening pressure during LP (must be done with patient in lateral decubitus, legs extended, relaxed); attributing papilledema to hypertension alone; ignoring pulsatile tinnitus.

### 3. Pathophysiology — The "Why" Behind The Findings

**The story of IIH development:**

The core problem is **impaired CSF absorption** at the arachnoid granulations (or, in some cases, elevated cerebral venous sinus pressure). Normally, CSF is produced at ~0.3-0.5 mL/min and absorbed into the venous system via arachnoid villi. In IIH, for reasons still debated, absorption is functionally reduced. This leads to a state of **CSF overaccumulation** (not overproduction – production is normal).

- **Why obesity?** Adipose tissue secretes multiple cytokines and hormones that alter CSF dynamics. Increased intra-abdominal pressure elevates central venous pressure, which in turn increases cerebral venous sinus pressure, reducing the pressure gradient for CSF absorption. Weight loss >10% can restore normal dynamics.
- **Why women?** Hormonal factors (androgen-to-estrogen ratio, leptin) influence arachnoid granulation function. Some cases are triggered by oral contraceptives, tetracyclines, or vitamin A derivatives – these drugs may alter CSF outflow resistance.

**Mechanism → key clinical sign:**

| Pathophys mechanism | Clinical sign | Why? |
|----------------------|---------------|------|
| Elevated CSF pressure transmitted to optic nerve sheath | Papilledema | Axoplasmic flow stasis at optic nerve head → swelling of nerve fibers → visible blurred disc margins, obliterated cup, hemorrhages |
| Stretching of meninges (tentorium, falx) by high-pressure CSF | Headache worse on lying flat | Meningeal traction increased when recumbent; pain fibers in cranial nerves (V, IX, X) are irritated |
| Transient ischemia of swollen optic nerve head | Transient visual obscurations | Postural drop in perfusion pressure across a swollen disc → temporary "gray-out" |
| High-pressure CSF pulsations transmitted to venous sinuses | Pulsatile tinnitus | CSF pressure wave mechanically compresses the transverse sinus, creating turbulent flow sound in middle ear |
| Chronic high ICP → shear stress on pituitary stalk | Endocrine disturbance (rare) | Can cause mild hyperprolactinemia (menstrual irregularities) or growth hormone suppression |

### 4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins)

| Category | Cause | Stem clue |
|----------|-------|-----------|
| **Idiopathic (most common)** | No identifiable cause | Young obese female, no meds, no thrombosis |
| **Medication-related** | Tetracyclines (doxycycline, minocycline), vitamin A/retinoids (isotretinoin), lithium, growth hormone, some oral contraceptives | "Started minocycline for acne 2 months ago" |
| **Venous sinus thrombosis** | Thrombus in transverse/sigmoid sinus → impaired CSF absorption | Sudden, severe headache + focal signs? Suspect if male, lean, or hypercoagulable state (OCP, pregnancy, malignancy, Factor V Leiden). MRV essential |
| **Systemic disease** | Obstructive sleep apnea, Addison's disease, hypoparathyroidism, chronic kidney disease (with anemia) | Snoring, daytime sleepiness; hyperpigmentation (Addison's); tetany (hypopara) |
| **Mass lesion mimicker** | Brain tumor, hydrocephalus, chronic meningitis | Focal signs, progressive headache, altered GCS, seizures – these are not IIH! |

**Most common cause:** Idiopathic (hence the name), but always exclude venous sinus thrombosis before labelling as such.

### 5. Investigations — What, Why, What It Means

**Diagnostic criteria:** Modified Dandy criteria (2013) – must have:
- Symptoms of raised ICP (headache, pulsatile tinnitus, transient visual obscurations)
- Signs (papilledema) – note: can be absent in some cases
- Normal neuroimaging (CT/MRI) with no mass, hydrocephalus, or structural lesion
- Normal CSF composition (no cells, normal protein/glucose)
- Elevated CSF opening pressure: **≥250 mmH₂O** (some sources use >200 in symptomatic, but >250 is diagnostic in children/adults)

**Investigations step-by-step:**

1. **MRI brain with MR venography (MRV)** – First step in ED if papilledema found.
   - **What it shows:** Excludes mass, hydrocephalus, empty sella (common in IIH but not diagnostic), flattened pituitary, distended optic nerve sheath, transverse sinus stenosis (often present, can be primary or secondary). MRV to exclude venous sinus thrombosis.
   - **Why:** Must rule out a cause requiring neurosurgery. MRV is mandatory – missed thrombosis is a medico-legal risk.

2. **Lumbar puncture (LP) with opening pressure** – Must be done immediately if imaging normal.
   - **How:** Lateral decubitus, legs extended, patient relaxed. Measure opening pressure with manometer. Record CSF for cell count, protein, glucose.
   - **Interpretation:** Opening pressure >250 mmH₂O confirms IIH (if CSF normal). If pressure 200-250, consider borderline. Normal CSF composition rules out meningitis or subarachnoid hemorrhage.
   - **Pattern recognition:** The single finding that confirms IIH is **elevated opening pressure with normal imaging and normal CSF**. Normal pressure does NOT rule out – IIH can be intermittent. Repeat if strong suspicion.

3. **Formal visual fields (Humphrey perimetry)** – Baseline and serial monitoring.
   - **What it shows:** Enlarged blind spot (early), arcuate defects, nasal step, concentric constriction. Vision loss is often silent until late.

4. **Lumbar puncture opening pressure revisited:** Many juniors forget to measure opening pressure. **Do not disconnect the manometer too early** – wait 60 seconds for stabilization. Readings in sitting are unreliable (gravity adds ~20 cmH₂O).

### 6. Management — Approach & Reasoning

**Immediate (first hour, usually in ED/neurology assessment)**
- **Therapeutic lumbar puncture** – Drain 20-30 mL CSF until closing pressure <200 mmH₂O. This provides immediate headache relief and reduces papilledema. Do not delay if vision threatened.
- **Start acetazolamide** 500 mg PO BD (or 1g IV if severe). Mechanism: carbonic anhydrase inhibitor reduces CSF production by ~50%. Goal: 1-2 g/day, max 4 g/day. Side effects: metabolic acidosis, paresthesias, metallic taste, renal stones.
- **Analgesia** – Paracetamol 1g QDS, avoid opioids (can lower threshold for nausea and mask worsening headache). If severe headache, consider IV diuretics (furosemide 40mg) as adjunct to reduce ICP.

**Ward (24-72 hours)**
- **Monitor visual acuity, colour vision, and formal visual fields** daily. Any deterioration (drop of >2 lines on Snellen, new field defect) → urgent ophthalmology/neurosurgery consult.
- **Optimize acetazolamide dose** – escalate by 500 mg every 2-3 days until response or intolerance. Switch to topiramate 25 mg BD (also carbonic anhydrase inhibitor, plus headache prophylaxis) if acetazolamide side effects intolerable.
- **Weight loss counselling** – Start now. Goal: 5-10% body weight over 6 months. Refer to obesity clinic / dietitian. This is the only disease-modifying treatment.
- **Consider stopping exacerbating drugs** – tetracyclines, retinoids, lithium.

**Long-term (outpatient follow-up)**
- **Medical management continues** – acetazolamide or topiramate for 6-12 months, then taper if vision stable and weight reduced. Repeat LP if symptoms recur.
- **Surgical options (when medical fails):**
  - **Optic nerve sheath fenestration** (faster, for vision loss)
  - **CSF shunting (lumboperitoneal or ventriculoperitoneal)** – for refractory headache and ICP. Risk of shunt infection, obstruction, overdrainage.
  - **Venous sinus stenting** (if transverse sinus stenosis confirmed on venography) – emerging therapy.
- **Pitfalls to avoid:**
  - Do **NOT** use steroids – they can increase ICP and worsen outcomes (paradoxical effect).
  - Do **NOT** delay LP for a minor headache – vision loss is painless and progressive.
  - Do **NOT** discharge without visual field baseline.
  - Do **NOT** co-prescribe acetazolamide with topiramate (additive acidosis risk) – triage to one.

### 7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison Table

| Feature | IIH | Cerebral Venous Sinus Thrombosis (CVST) | Chronic Migraine (without aura) | Brain Tumor (e.g., meningioma) | Subarachnoid Hemorrhage |
|---------|-----|-----------------------------------------|----------------------------------|---------------------------------|--------------------------|
| **Headache** | Diffuse, worse lying flat, pulsatile tinnitus | Sudden, thunderclap, may be progressive | Unilateral, throbbing, photophonophobia, unilateral | Progressive, morning vomiting, worse with cough | Sudden, worst-ever, "thunderclap" |
| **Papilledema** | Bilateral, often symmetric | Unilateral or bilateral (if later thrombosis) | Absent | May be unilateral if tumor compresses optic nerve | Absent (unless massive bleed) |
| **Imaging (CT/MRI)** | Normal brain, empty sella, left transverse sinus stenosis | Hyperdense sinus, "empty delta sign", edema on CTV | Normal | Mass effect, contrast enhancement | Subarachnoid/ventricular blood |
| **CSF OP** | >250 mmH₂O | Normal or elevated (but often >200) | Normal | Normal (unless hydrocephalus) | Normal or elevated (with blood) |
| **CSF composition** | Normal | May have RBCs, protein elevated | Normal | Normal | Consistent with blood (xanthochromia) |
| **Key differentiating feature** | Normal CSF, female obesity, no history of hypercoagulability | MRV shows thrombus; risk factors (OCP, pregnancy, thrombophilia) | Hx of migrations, no papilledema, normal OP | Focal neurology, mass on imaging | Subarachnoid blood on CT, thunderclap onset |

### 8. Complications — What If You Miss It?

**Permanent vision loss** – The most feared complication.
- **How it presents:** Initially asymptomatic visual field defects (arcuate, nasal step). Central vision preserved until late. Then: severe field constriction, acuity drop, eventually optic atrophy (disc becomes pale, swelling resolves).
- **Key signs:** Reduced colour vision, afferent pupillary defect if asymmetric, enlarged blind spot. Once optic atrophy sets in, intervention cannot reverse.
- **What to do:** Urgent LP, high-dose acetazolamide, neurosurgical referral for optic nerve sheath fenestration within hours.

**Headache disability** – Chronic headache affects quality of life and work.
- **Presentation:** Daily tension-like or migraine-like headache despite medical therapy. May persist even after ICP normalized.
- **Key signs:** No papilledema, normal opening pressure during exacerbation. May indicate central sensitization.
- **What to do:** Multidisciplinary approach (pain clinic, amitriptyline, topiramate, avoidance of medication overuse).

**False localizing signs** – Rare.
- **Examples:** Sixth nerve palsy (bilateral or unilateral) due to stretch over petrous ridge. Transient symptoms resolve with ICP lowering.

**Late-presentation pattern (the "miss" stem):**
> A 45-year-old obese woman presents with progressive vision loss in one eye over 6 months. Fundoscopy reveals pale discs with sharp margins. She recalls a headache that resolved spontaneously. Opening pressure is normal. This is **end-stage IIH** with optic atrophy. No treatment can restore vision. The headache resolved because ICP normalized after permanent damage. Tragic but testable.

### 9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls

**Mnemonics:**

**"VOICE"** – The four cardinal symptoms of IIH (classic stem set-up):
- **V** – Visual obscurations (transient)
- **O** – Obesity
- **I** – Intracranial headache (worse on lying down)
- **C** – Cranial nerve palsy (VI most common)
- **E** – Ear (pulsatile tinnitus)

**"DON'T BE FLAT"** – What to avoid when suspecting IIH:
- **D** – Delay LP
- **O** – Omit MRVenogram (miss thrombosis)
- **N** – No steroids (they worsen)
- **T** – Treat BP instead of ICP (hypertensive papilledema vs IIH)
- **B** – Bypass formal visual fields
- **E** – Expect normal pressure in all cases (intermittent elevation)
- **FLAT** – Fontanelle flat? Not relevant in adults – but helps in kids.

**Clinical Pearls:**
1. **"If the disc is swollen, always check the opposite eye"** – IIH almost always bilateral; unilateral papilledema suggests tumor or thrombosis.
2. **Pulsatile tinnitus that disappears when you compress the jugular vein** (S.S. sign) suggests high-flow venous sinus stenosis – common in IIH.
3. **Opening pressure ≥250 mmH₂O is diagnostic but treat pressures >200 if symptomatic** – some patients have symptomatic IIH with pressure 200-250, especially if they have empty sella or tortuous optic sheath.
4. **Weight loss >10% cures about 60% of mild-to-moderate IIH** – this is the only disease-modifying intervention. Acetazolamide buys time.
5. **Do not measure opening pressure in sitting** – you'll get falsely high (by ~15-20 cmH₂O). Always decubitus.
6. **IIH can cause low-pressure headache too** – after LP, overdrainage → spinal headache (worse sitting, better lying). Differentiate from recurrence.
7. **Beware of "pseudopapilledema" (optic disc drusen)** – these are buried deposits in optic nerve head that mimic swelling. Use ultrasound or autofluorescence to distinguish. IIH papilledema shows spontaneous venous pulsations in early stages (absent in drusen).

**Common ward mistakes:**
- Applying anti-hypertensives to "treat the headache" – BP is not elevated in IIH unless pain-induced.
- Ordering CT head and stopping (forgetting MRV) – you miss thrombosis.
- Giving steroids "to reduce swelling" – lethal mistake that increases ICP.
- Discharging a patient with new papilledema without an LP – a classic exam failure.

### 10. Escalation Criteria

- **Call senior (neurology / ophthalmology / neurosurgery) immediately if:**
  - **Sudden vision loss** (acuity drop >2 lines Snellen or new field defect) – needs surgical fenestration within hours.
  - **GCS drop <15** – suggests herniation (rare in IIH but possible if tonometry is extreme >500).
  - **Seizure** – think venous sinus thrombosis extension or cortical vein thrombosis (call stroke team).
  - **Headache unresponsive to LP and acetazolamide** after 24 hours – consider shunt or stenting.
  - **NEWS2 ≥5** (especially if new neurological sign or lactate >2) – rule out sepsis or meningitis (coincident infection can worsen ICP).

- **When to call ICU:**
  - **GCS ≤8** (needs airway protection and ICP monitoring).
  - **Intractable vomiting** with electrolytes disturbance.
  - **Severe metabolic acidosis** from acetazolamide (pH <7.2, HCO₃ <12) – stop drug, give bicarbonate, consider topiramate instead.

- **When to call crash team:**
  - **Cardiorespiratory arrest** (likely unrelated to IIH, but can follow vomiting with aspiration).
  - **Sudden loss of consciousness** (consider acute hydrocephalus from shunt malfunction in known IIH patients with VP shunts).
